name: Granite State
description_short: State-chartered credit union based in Manchester, NH, serving members statewide since 1945. Offers traditional credit union membership and business lending services.
description_long: Granite State Credit Union (GSCU) was founded in 1945 by John Edward Grace as Utility Workers Credit Union, initially serving public service company employees and Manchester Transit Authority workers. The organization was renamed in 1972 and expanded its charter to serve anyone living in New Hampshire. GSCU is a state-chartered credit union headquartered in Manchester with branches throughout New Hampshire, and maintains membership in both the New Hampshire Credit Union League (NHCUL) and the Credit Union National Association (CUNA). As a credit union, GSCU provides standard banking and financial services to its members. The organization also offers business lending through Octant Business Services LLC, a credit union service organization based in Marlborough, Massachusetts, expanding its reach beyond consumer banking into commercial lending. GSCU's distinguishing feature is its deep historical roots in New Hampshire, with over 75 years of community service and a founding story rooted in the trust-based leadership of John Edward Grace and his wife Betty. The credit union merged with Acorn Credit Union in late 2003 or early 2004, consolidating its presence in the state. The organization's legacy is commemorated in exhibits at America's Credit Union Museum on Notre Dame Avenue in Manchester. The Wikipedia article notes that the Products and Services section is empty and marked as needing expansion since July 2010. This lack of publicly available information about specific offerings and terms makes it difficult for consumers to evaluate GSCU's competitive positioning without visiting their website directly at www.gscu.org. Many banks offer installment loans with fixed monthly payments, giving borrowers a clear payoff timeline.
services: Credit union membership for New Hampshire residents
Business lending through Octant Business Services LLC
Access to New Hampshire Credit Union League services
Multiple branch banking throughout New Hampshire
pros: Federally insured credit union member of CUNA (Credit Union National Association)
Over 75 years of community service and established local presence in New Hampshire
State-chartered with expanded membership eligibility (all New Hampshire residents)
Offers business lending through dedicated service organization (Octant Business Services)
Multiple branch locations throughout New Hampshire
Member of New Hampshire Credit Union League for additional protections and services
Historical recognition and legacy exhibits at America's Credit Union Museum
cons: Public information about products and services is minimal—Wikipedia article's Products section is empty and unfilled since 2010
No detailed information available about rates, fees, loan terms, or membership requirements from provided sources
Limited online presence described in available documentation; specific offerings must be researched at www.gscu.org
Cannot verify current payday alternative loan products or APR caps from available sources
Geographically limited to New Hampshire membership
best_for: New Hampshire residents seeking traditional credit union banking with local community roots
Small business owners and entrepreneurs in New England seeking commercial lending
Individuals valuing membership in established, federally-insured financial institutions
diagnosis: Granite State Credit Union is best for New Hampshire residents seeking traditional credit union banking from an established local institution with over 75 years of community history. The main caveat is that publicly available information about specific products, services, rates, and terms is extremely limited, requiring direct contact with the credit union to evaluate whether they offer payday alternative loans or meet specific borrowing needs.
typical_results_timeline: Membership approval and account opening typically takes 1-3 business days. Loan decisions are usually faster than traditional banks.
